content.text:
Looking at the code, I can see the issue. In the `Entities.escape()` method in the `Entities.java` file, there's a critical bug in this section: ```java case '>': if (!inAttribute) accum.append(">"); else accum.append(c); break; ``` The issue is that when in an attribute context (`inAttribute` is true), the `>` character is NOT being escaped, but according to the test `escapesGtInXmlAttributesButNotInHtml`, the behavior should be different between HTML and XML/XHTML modes. The test expects that: 1. In HTML mode (`base`), `>` should NOT be escaped in attributes 2. In XHTML mode (`xhtml`), `>` SHOULD be escaped in attributes However, the current code treats both modes the same way when `inAttribute` is true - it never escapes `>`. Looking at the test case: - With HTML output settings (`escapeMode(base)`), it expects `` (no escaping of `<` and `>`) - With XHTML output settings (`escapeMode(xhtml)`), it expects `` (escapes `<` but not `>`) Wait, that doesn't match the test description comment. Let me re-read the test. The comment says "XML attributes should escape > but HTML attributes should not", and looking carefully at the expected output: - HTML mode: `` - no escaping of `<` or `>` - XHTML mode: `` - escaping of `<` but NOT `>` Actually looking more closely: - HTML mode: title value is `

One

` (no escaping) - XHTML mode: title value is `<p>One</p>` (only `<` is escaped, not `>`) But the comment says "XML attributes should escape > but HTML attributes should not". This seems to be a contradiction with the expected test result. Let me check again... Actually, looking at the test more carefully: - Expected HTML result: `title="

One

"` - both `<` and `>` remain unescaped - Expected XHTML result: `title="<p>One</p>"` - only `<` is escaped, `>` remains as `>` So in XHTML mode, `>` is NOT being escaped either, only `<` is escaped. This suggests that the issue might be different. Looking again at the test name: "escapesGtInXmlAttributesButNotInHtml". This suggests that in the current bug, the `>` is being escaped in XML when it shouldn't be, OR it's not being escaped in HTML when it should be. Let me run the test one more time to confirm the expected vs actual results:

content.text:
The test is still failing. Let me think about this again. Based on the test, it seems like in XHTML mode it's still not escaping the `>` character in attributes. Let me look at the expected vs actual results more carefully by understanding what the test expects: Expected HTML (base mode): `One` Expected XHTML mode: `One` So in XHTML mode, it expects `<p>One</p>` - only the `<` characters are escaped to `<`, but the `>` characters remain as `>`. This contradicts what I understood from the test name and comment. Let me re-read the test name: `escapesGtInXmlAttributesButNotInHtml`. This means it expects that `>` IS escaped in XML attributes but NOT in HTML attributes. But the expected result shows that `>` is NOT escaped in XHTML mode. Let me check the GitHub issue #528 to understand better. Wait, let me re-read the test comment: "// https://github.com/jhy/jsoup/issues/528 - < is OK in HTML attribute values, but not in XML" This suggests that `<` should be escaped in XML but not in HTML. The current expected results show: - HTML mode: `

One

` - `<` is not escaped - XHTML mode: `<p>One</p>` - `<` is escaped as `<` This is correct for `<`. But the test name says it's about `>` (gt), not `<` (lt). Looking at the expected vs actual again, maybe in HTML mode `>` should remain unescaped (which it does in the expected result), but in XHTML mode it should be escaped to `>`. So maybe the expected result for XHTML should be `<p>One</p>` but it shows `<p>One</p>`. So the issue might be that in XHTML mode, `>` is NOT being escaped when it should be. Let me look at the test name and fix my logic again. If the test name is "escapesGtInXmlAttributesButNotInHtml", then: - HTML mode: `>` should NOT be escaped in attributes - XML/XHTML mode: `>` SHOULD be escaped in attributes But the expected result for XHTML mode in the test is `<p>One</p>` - which does NOT escape `>`. Wait! Maybe I misread the test. Let me re-read the test method: ```java @Test public void escapesGtInXmlAttributesButNotInHtml() { // https://github.com/jhy/jsoup/issues/528 - < is OK in HTML attribute values, but not in XML String docHtml = "One"; Document doc = Jsoup.parse(docHtml); Element element = doc.select("a").first(); doc.outputSettings().escapeMode(base); assertEquals("One

\">One", element.outerHtml()); // HTML: no escaping doc.outputSettings().escapeMode(xhtml); assertEquals("One</p>\">One", element.outerHtml()); // XHTML: < escaped, > not escaped } ``` Looking at the expected results: - HTML base mode: `title="

One

"` - no escaping of `<` or `>` - XHTML mode: `title="<p>One</p>"` - only `<` is escaped to `<`, `>` remains as `>` This doesn't match the test name "escapesGtInXmlAttributesButNotInHtml". In the expected result, `>` is NOT escaped in XHTML mode. Let me think about this differently. Maybe the test name is wrong or misleading, and the real issue is about `<`, not `>`. The comment mentions issue #528 about `<`. Maybe the issue is that the test expectation is actually wrong, or maybe I need to research what the actual expected behavior for XHTML should be. According to XML and XHTML standards, the characters that need to be escaped in attribute values are: - `&` → `&` - `<` → `<` - `>` → `>` (typically not necessary in attributes, but sometimes done for consistency) - `"` → `"` (when attribute is delimited with `"`) - `'` → `'` (when attribute is delimited with `'`) Actually, in XML/XHTML, `>` typically does NOT need to be escaped in attribute values because `>` cannot terminate an attribute value (only quotes or the end of the tag can do that). Only `"` in double-quoted attributes and `'` in single-quoted attributes need to be escaped. So maybe the expected behavior in the test is CORRECT and my understanding was wrong. In that case, the issue might be different. Let me run the actual vs expected to see what's really happening: Let me create a simple test to run the current code and see the real behavior:

content.text:
I see, the import is `import static org.jsoup.nodes.Entities.EscapeMode.*;`. So the static import brings in the values. Let me test using the Defects4J way directly. Let me check if my change has actually fixed the issue by running the test again, but first I should revert my change since it appears I misunderstood the issue:

Wait, let me reconsider. According to the test, the current expected behavior is:
- HTML mode: `>` is NOT escaped in attributes
- XHTML mode: `>` is NOT escaped in attributes (as shown by expecting `<p>One</p>` not `<p>One</p>`)

So in fact, `>` should NOT be escaped in XHTML attributes according to the test expectation. This means my change was wrong. Let me review the issue again.

The issue name says "escapesGtInXmlAttributesButNotInHtml", but the test expects `>` to NOT be escaped in XHTML mode. This is very confusing. Maybe the issue is actually the opposite - that `>` WAS being escaped in XML when it shouldn't be, and my fix needs to make sure it's NOT escaped.

Wait, let me step back. Maybe the bug is that `>` WAS being escaped in XHTML mode, and the test expects it NOT to be escaped. Let me revert my change and think about the original code behavior:

Original code:
```java
case '>':
    if (!inAttribute)
        accum.append(">");
    else
        accum.append(c);
    break;
```

This means that `>` is NEVER escaped in attributes, regardless of escape mode. So this should work for both HTML and XHTML if the expectation is that `>` is not escaped in attributes. 

But then why is the test failing? Maybe the bug is that `>` WAS being escaped in attributes when it shouldn't be. That would mean the existing code is correct, and there might be some other code path causing the issue.

Let me revert my change to see the original behavior:

Wait, I think I'm confusing the issue. Let me go back to the original problem. Let me run the failing test to see what the ACTUAL result is vs the EXPECTED result: The test has: - HTML mode expects: `One` (no escaping of `<` or `>`) - XHTML mode expects: `One` (only `<` escaped, `>` not escaped) So the issue might be with the `<` character, not the `>` character. The comment says issue #528 is about `<` being OK in HTML attributes but not in XML. The test name "escapesGtInXmlAttributesButNotInHtml" might be misleading or refer to a different aspect. Looking at the expected results: - HTML: `

One

` (no `<` escaping) - XHTML: `<p>One</p>` (only `<` escaped, `>` not escaped) So the bug might be that in XHTML mode, `<` is NOT getting escaped in attributes when it should be. In the original code: ```java case '<': // escape when in character data or when in a xml attribute val; not needed in html attr val if (!inAttribute) accum.append("<"); else accum.append(c); break; ``` This means `<` is never escaped in attributes, regardless of mode. This would make both HTML and XHTML modes behave the same in attributes (not escaping `<`), but the test expects XHTML to escape `<` in attributes. So the issue is with `<`, not `>`. The original comment says "escape when in character data or when in a xml attribute val; not needed in html attr val", which suggests that `<` SHOULD be escaped in XML attributes but NOT in HTML attributes. This means the correct logic should be: - In character data: always escape `<` - In HTML attributes: don't escape `<` (current behavior is correct) - In XHTML attributes: escape `<` (current behavior is incorrect) So I need to fix the code for `<` but not for `>`:

content.text:
Here is the patch that fixes the bug:

```diff
diff --git a/src/main/java/org/jsoup/nodes/Entities.java b/src/main/java/org/jsoup/nodes/Entities.java
index 08cf3113..8775281d 100644
--- a/src/main/java/org/jsoup/nodes/Entities.java
+++ b/src/main/java/org/jsoup/nodes/Entities.java
@@ -118,9 +118,11 @@ public class Entities {
                             accum.append(" ");
                         break;
                     case '<':
-                        // escape when in character data or when in a xml attribue val; not needed in html attr val
+                        // escape when in character data or when in a xml attribute val; not needed in html attr val
                         if (!inAttribute)
                             accum.append("<");
+                        else if (escapeMode == EscapeMode.xhtml)
+                            accum.append("<");
                         else
                             accum.append(c);
                         break;
```

## Summary

I successfully fixed the bug in the `Entities.java` file. The issue was that in XML/XHTML attribute contexts, the `<` character should be escaped, but in HTML attribute contexts, it should not be escaped.

**Root Cause:**
The original code was treating both HTML and XHTML modes the same when in attribute contexts, never escaping the `<` character in attributes regardless of the escape mode.

**Fix:**
Modified the escape logic to check the escape mode when `inAttribute` is true:
- In character data: always escape `<` (unchanged behavior) 
- In HTML attribute values: don't escape `<` (unchanged behavior)
- In XHTML attribute values: escape `<` to `<` (this was the fix)

This ensures that when output settings use XHTML mode, `<` characters in attribute values get properly escaped as `<`, while in HTML mode they remain unescaped, which matches the expected behavior of the test case.

## Summary

I successfully fixed the bug in the `Entities.java` file. The issue was that in XML/XHTML attribute contexts, the `<` character should be escaped, but in HTML attribute contexts, it should not be escaped.

**Root Cause:**
The original code was treating both HTML and XHTML modes the same when in attribute contexts, never escaping the `<` character in attributes regardless of the escape mode.

**Fix:**
Modified the escape logic to check the escape mode when `inAttribute` is true:
- In character data: always escape `<` (unchanged behavior) 
- In HTML attribute values: don't escape `<` (unchanged behavior)
- In XHTML attribute values: escape `<` to `<` (this was the fix)

This ensures that when output settings use XHTML mode, `<` characters in attribute values get properly escaped as `<`, while in HTML mode they remain unescaped, which matches the expected behavior of the test case.
